data.title: China‚Äôs manufacturing powerhouse Guangdong exports record chip equipment
data.description:
Exports of chip-manufacturing equipment, electric vehicles (EVs) and other high-value goods from China‚Äôs southern Guangdong province rose to a record high last year of over 1 trillion yuan (US$140 billion), highlighting the country‚Äôs progress in its quest for technological self-sufficiency in an ongoing tech war with the US.
The country‚Äôs leading manufacturing hub exported semiconductor equipment worth more than 800 billion yuan in 2025, an increase of nearly 20 per cent from a year earlier, said Zhang Jinsong, head of Guangdong‚Äôs Department of Commerce, at an event on Monday.
Accelerating technological self-reliance is one of the province‚Äôs main priorities this year.
Meanwhile, exports of EVs, lithium batteries and solar products from Guangdong also hit an all-time high, rising more than 30 per cent year on year to over 200 billion yuan.

Guangdong, home to tech and EV giants such as Huawei Technologies, Tencent Holdings, BYD and Xpeng, ‚Äúhas enhanced the security, stability and reliability of the industrial chain‚Äù, said Zeng Jinze, head of the province‚Äôs Department of Industry and Information Technology, at the same event.
He said Huawei‚Äôs Ascend 910C had become ‚Äúthe mainstream computing chip product in China‚Äù.
Zeng also highlighted the strides made by the automotive chip sector. He said that 100 per cent of the chips used by the province‚Äôs car industry were designed with domestic tools, up from less than 5 per cent in the past, without specifying a time frame.
Shenzhen-based Huawei has been actively pursuing self-reliance in semiconductors among other areas where it has been subject to sanctions by Washington since 2019.
The Ascend 910C AI accelerator delivered around 60 per cent of the performance of Nvidia‚Äôs H100 on reference tasks, according to a DeepSeek study. Huawei was set to double the chip‚Äôs output to 600,000 this year, Bloomberg reported in September.
Chinese carmakers are gradually shifting from Nvidia chips to domestic alternatives used for driver assistance systems and intelligent cockpits.
Last year, Guangzhou-based Xpeng switched to its in-house Turing AI chip for its new models. The company claimed the chip‚Äôs computing power was equal to roughly three Nvidia Orin X chips.
Shenzhen-based BYD, which overtook Tesla in 2025 to become the world‚Äôs top electric vehicle seller, also operates a semiconductor unit.
The company plans to deploy self-made chips in its cars by June this year. Currently, BYD sources chips mainly from Nvidia and China‚Äôs Horizon Robotics.

data.title: China‚Äôs Alibaba, Moonshot claim latest AI models challenge OpenAI, Google
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding and Moonshot AI have unveiled their latest flagship artificial intelligence models, narrowing the gap with US industry leaders OpenAI and Google DeepMind.
The new models from two of China‚Äôs leading AI developers mark the first major Chinese model releases of this year, as industry observers continue to closely monitor the gap between the US and China in developing cutting-edge foundational models.
Alibaba Cloud announced its biggest-ever model, Qwen3-Max-Thinking, on Monday, which it said boasted stronger agentic and tool use capabilities. The model was released on Alibaba‚Äôs official cloud-services platform and chatbot website, with the model set to be available on its new consumer Qwen app ‚Äúsoon‚Äù, it said.
Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud computing unit of Alibaba Group Holding, which owns the Post.
‚ÄúThis is our best model so far,‚Äù said Zheng Chujie, an Alibaba researcher, on social media. ‚ÄúWe have ‚Ä¶ put massive efforts [into] improving real-world user experience.‚Äù
The model is the latest in the Hangzhou-based company‚Äôs Qwen3 series, first released in May, which initially consisted of models ranging from 600 million to 235 billion parameters and has since reached models of more than 1 trillion parameters, including Qwen3-Max-Thinking.

Parameters are the variables encoding an AI model‚Äôs intelligence, which are adjusted during training. In general, a higher number of parameters means a more capable model, although more computational resources are also needed.
In a blog post, Alibaba said that the new model‚Äôs performance stemmed from a further increase in model size and ‚Äúsubstantial computational resources for reinforcement learning‚Äù.
Alibaba said it used new techniques to improve the efficiency of ‚Äútest-time scaling‚Äù ‚Äì adding more computational resources during model deployment ‚Äì by focusing more computational resources on unresolved uncertainties, thereby boosting performance without significant computational overhead.
On internal tests, Alibaba said the model achieved ‚Äúcomparable‚Äù performance to leading US models such as Anthropic‚Äôs Claude-Opus-4.5 and Google DeepMind‚Äôs Gemini 3 Pro across 19 benchmarks.
However, some Qwen fans expressed disappointment that the model had not been open-sourced. While Qwen is one of the world‚Äôs most popular open-source model families, Alibaba has kept its biggest and most powerful Max models closed-source so far and used them to power its Qwen app.
Meanwhile, Alibaba-backed start-up Moonshot AI announced its newest model Kimi K2.5 on Tuesday, calling it the world‚Äôs most powerful open-source model.
Notably, the new model introduces a feature called agent swarm, allowing developers to better tackle complex coding tasks by using up to 100 subagents that execute workflows in parallel.
The company said the feature would greatly benefit programmers using Kimi models, which the Beijing-based start-up has pitched as cheaper alternatives to industry leader Anthropic‚Äôs Claude models in the key AI application market of coding.
The new model marks the first time Moonshot has added multimodal capabilities to its products, enabling processing and generation of video and images in addition to text.

Both Moonshot and the Qwen team have been proponents of scaling laws in AI ‚Äì the theory that adding computational power and data will deliver reliable gains in model performance.
In a road map unveiled in September, the Qwen team announced the goal of scaling up its models to more than 10 trillion parameters to keep pace with US counterparts that were already training models approaching that size.
However, Qwen team leader Lin Junyang hinted at an industry event earlier this month that computational constraints posed a major challenge, as resources were being tied up in daily delivery instead of being used for research and development.

data.title: Hong Kong retiree spiked soft drinks with urine in year-long ‚Äòprank‚Äô
data.description:
A former property agent has admitted planting urine-spiked soft drinks among other goods in supermarkets repeatedly for more than a year, telling a Hong Kong court he had developed depression since his divorce and retirement.
Kowloon City Court heard that Franklin Lo Kim-ngai, 63, marked his discontent with supermarket staff by doctoring Coca-Cola Plus and 7-Up drinks with his urine and placing them in Wellcome and ParknShop outlets across the city between July 21, 2024, and August 6, last year.
He pleaded guilty on Tuesday to administering a noxious substance with intent to injure and a second count of attempting to commit the offence. The crime is punishable by up to three years in jail under the Offences Against the Person Ordinance.
Lo lost emotional support from his family following his parents‚Äô death and separation from his ex-wife and son, who stopped contacting him after emigrating overseas, his lawyer told the court.
The court heard the defendant, who previously had a clear record, had hoped to play a ‚Äúprank‚Äù and cause trouble for Wellcome employees after having a quarrel with them.
Before Lo‚Äôs arrest, Swire Coca-Cola had received multiple reports about the discovery of urine-spiked soft drinks at five Wellcome and ParknShop outlets.
A nine-year-old boy felt unwell after consuming a bottle of Coca-Cola Plus from the Wellcome branch at Mong Kok‚Äôs Union Park Centre on July 18, 2025. He was sent to hospital for treatment, although he was discharged the same day without lasting symptoms.
Further inquiries revealed Lo had visited the supermarkets and put the contaminated drinks on the shelves.
After his arrest on August 9, Lo said he transgressed the law because Wellcome staff made him unhappy.
Lo said in a letter to the court that he was depressed and had vented his frustration by inappropriate means, adding that he had thought of taking his life after his crime came to light.
Other letters written by the defendant‚Äôs relatives described him as a diligent and polite man who was very regretful.
The defence urged the court to treat the case as a one-off incident and give Lo a chance to reintegrate into society.
Magistrate Andrew Mok Tze-chung highlighted the gravity of the offence, but agreed to consider alternative sentencing options, including community service, probation and a hospital order, given the ‚Äúspecial‚Äù circumstances of the case.
Lo, who has been denied bail since he first appeared in court in August, will remain at Siu Lam Psychiatric Centre in Tuen Mun, pending sentence on February 10.

data.title: Hong Kong records US$95 million worth of luxury home deals in a single day
data.description:
Hong Kong‚Äôs property market on Monday logged HK$740 million (US$94.9 million) worth of luxury home deals, one of the city‚Äôs largest single-day hauls, as the residential segment shakes off a multi-year slump.
Records from the Land Registry showed that five upscale homes ‚Äì from areas such as Mid-Levels, Wan Chai and Sai Kung ‚Äì changed hands in deals valued between HK$93 million and HK$238 million.
The flurry of luxury home deals comes amid a recovery in Hong Kong‚Äôs residential property segment, as affluent homebuyers and investors aggressively snapped up trophy assets.
Recent deals were viewed as a signal that the number of attractively priced luxury homes could be dwindling, which led to a rush in deals being completed, according to Ricacorp Properties.
‚ÄúThe supply of ultra-luxury properties in Hong Kong is structurally extremely limited to begin with,‚Äù said Derek Chan Hoi-chiu, head of research at Ricacorp Properties. ‚ÄúFew new prime locations or rare properties are launched each year, and many high-quality older buildings are being held for the long term.‚Äù
As of November, the prices of homes with areas of at least 1,076 sq ft rose by about 1.35 per cent compared with the end of 2024, according to official data. That was still lower by about 20 per cent from their peak in October 2021.
‚ÄúWith property prices significantly down from historical highs, mainland capital has been strongly drawn back,‚Äù Chan said. ‚ÄúBuyers are concerned that the window for acquiring ‚Äògood-quality properties‚Äô is closing. Prices have already stabilised and are starting to rise, and delaying a purchase would only make it more expensive later.‚Äù

The largest among Monday‚Äôs transactions involved a house at Wan Chai‚Äôs Black‚Äôs Link, with a saleable area of 5,104 sq ft, estimated at HK$46,630 per square foot.
The property features terraces, a swimming pool, car ports as well as guest car parks, according to official records. The deal also included the surrounding land.
The buyer was identified as an entity named New Eden Capital, which shelled out HK$190.5 million more than the HK$47.5 million paid by previous owner Eastern Will Investment in 1999.
In Mid-Levels, a four-room, 3,699 sq ft unit at the High Peak development on No 23 Po Shan Road went for HK$162.63 million, or HK$43,972 per square foot, according to official data and information from agents. The buyer registered the property under a Chinese name, which reads as Wu Shibin, a director of Zheng Ao Industrial, government documents showed.
Another big-ticket transaction involved a HK$138.42 million, four-bedroom flat at the 21 Borrett Road project in Mid-Levels. The property, which has a 2,256 sq ft saleable area, which translated to HK$61,353 per square foot, made it the most expensive among the deals in terms of per square foot price. The buyer was an entity called LFT Asset Management, according to Land Registry records.
A house with a saleable area of 3,878 sq ft at Turtle Cove in Tai Tam, in the affluent Southern district, went for HK$108 million. The buyer was identified as Vitality Holdings Limited. The property ‚Äì with a terrace, patio and a service yard ‚Äì was bought for HK$49.5 million in 1998 by Welltop Limited.
Meanwhile, a three-bedroom flat at the Estoril Court in Mid-Levels, with a 2,888 sq ft saleable area, was bought for HK$31,233 per square foot, which made it the fifth most expensive luxury home deal on Monday. The joint buyers paid HK$92.8 million for the unit, records showed. The previous owners acquired the property for HK$32.98 million in 2006.

data.title: Man arrested as Hong Kong police seize illegal fireworks worth HK$1 million
data.description:
Hong Kong police have charged a man with illegal possession of fireworks and firecrackers after seizing a haul worth HK$1 million (US$128,200) from a shipping container in Tuen Mun, three weeks ahead of the Lunar New Year.
Officers from the New Territories North regional anti-triad unit found 58 boxes containing 800kg (1,764lbs) of pyrotechnics during a raid in Tai Lam Chung at about 4pm on Monday, the force said.
Senior Inspector Kwok Man-wai said police found the goods in a container rented by the 37-year-old suspect, adding that he was scheduled to appear at Fanling Court on February 12.
‚ÄúPolice were committed to an intelligence-led operation to combat fireworks- and firecracker-related offences, intercepting the goods before they flowed into the market,‚Äù Kwok said.
‚ÄúThese items are extremely likely to cause fires or other serious accidents, posing a major threat to personal and public safety.‚Äù
Those convicted of illegal possession of fireworks or firecrackers under the Dangerous Goods Ordinance face a maximum penalty of 12 months‚Äô imprisonment and a HK$200,000 fine.
Under the Summary Offences Ordinance, anyone who sets off fireworks that endanger people or cause damage to property can be fined HK$2,000 and imprisoned for up to three months.
‚ÄúPolice would like to take this opportunity to appeal to the public again during the festive celebration period not to purchase or use illegal fireworks and firecrackers,‚Äù Kwok said.

data.title: Hong Kong to deny teachers practising certificates with insufficient training hours
data.description:
Teachers who fail to complete the required hours of professional development will be denied practising certificates, Hong Kong‚Äôs education authorities have said, ahead of meeting next week to discuss details of the proposed scheme.
Secretary for Education Christine Choi Yuk-lin said the proposal would also outline the requirements for rejoining the profession after registered teachers had left the sector for a period of time.
The proposed certificate was introduced as a new measure in Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu‚Äôs policy address in September. Under the plan, teachers will be required to obtain the certification from the Education Bureau ‚Äì valid for a set period and subject to regular renewal ‚Äì in addition to their existing teacher registration.
Choi told the Legislative Council‚Äôs education panel on Tuesday that the government would discuss the proposed certificate next week, with the meeting scheduled for February 6.
The panel will also address the Code of Practice for Private Schools, following reports that some local institutions had partnered with mainland Chinese agencies to offer classes. The government subsequently revoked or suspended the registrations of at least two schools.
Choi said the introduction of the certificate requiring regular renewal aimed to ensure all teachers were ‚Äúfit and proper‚Äù, thereby safeguarding professional teaching standards.

‚ÄúOur database shows that out of 160,000 registered teachers, only about 70,000 to 80,000 are currently practising. Some hold teaching licences but chose not to enter the profession for various reasons,‚Äù she said.
‚ÄúWe will also discuss how to address the situation of teachers rejoining the sector next week.‚Äù
Last September, Choi said authorities would consider a range of factors before deciding whether to grant the certificate, including whether applicants live in Hong Kong.
Deputy Secretary of the Education Bureau Verena Lau Wing-yin said the professional development requirement involved completing at least 150 hours of training over three years, including 30 hours of core training. She noted that most teachers had already met this requirement.
She said the 150-hour threshold would serve as a baseline for determining the amount of core training required.
According to the bureau‚Äôs website, the core training covers teachers‚Äô professional roles, values and conduct, as well as local, national and international education issues.
‚ÄúIf teachers cannot fulfil the teachers‚Äô training requirement, they will temporarily be unable to obtain the practising certificate,‚Äù Lau said.

data.title: MTR Corp raises record A$2 billion in first Australian dollar green bond sale
data.description:
Hong Kong‚Äôs rail giant has sold its first-ever Australian dollar-denominated green bonds for a record A$2 billion (US$1.4 billion) to fund environmentally friendly projects, marking the country‚Äôs largest order book.
The bonds, issued in two equal tranches, were oversubscribed more than sixfold by a variety of investors, the MTR Corporation said on Tuesday.
The offering represented the largest-ever Australian dollar corporate green bond and the largest dual-tranche corporate bond in the market. It was also the biggest corporate order book ever achieved in the country‚Äôs currency.
‚ÄúThe issuance of our inaugural Australian dollar green bonds is the next step in our efforts to maintain a highly diversified funding base to drive business growth,‚Äù MTR finance director Michael Fitzgerald said.
‚ÄúWe are delighted with the very positive response from investors, including insurance companies, official institutions, pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, asset managers, banks, securities houses and private banks from around the world.‚Äù
He added the proceeds would further strengthen the corporation‚Äôs financial foundation and enhance its flexibility to support the capital investment programme, which will enable it to build more vibrant communities and uphold service excellence.
An MTR spokesman said the bonds were issued in a dual-tranche transaction comprising a five-year tranche of A$1 billion and a 12-year tranche of A$1 billion, with coupon rates set at 4.886 per cent and 5.582 per cent, respectively.

He added that strong demand led to an oversubscription of 6.25 times, with a combined final order book of A$12.5 billion, and that the positive market response underscored investor confidence in the corporation‚Äôs commitment to sustainability.
ANZ, Deutsche Bank, MUFG and UBS acted as joint lead managers and joint bookrunners for the debt issuance, with ANZ appointed as the coordinator for environmental, social and governance (ESG) initiatives.
The MTR Corp has had a presence in Australia since 2009 and currently operates major rail franchises and metro systems in Melbourne and Sydney.
Last year, MTR secured a landmark HK$30 billion (US$3.86 billion) seven-year syndicated green term loan with the support of 57 banks worldwide in September, as well as a public bond issue that raised US$3 billion from a three-tranche offering amid a hot credit market in Asia in March.
In July, the rail giant and the Hong Kong government signed an agreement on the design and construction of a major rail link in the Northern Metropolis, fast-tracking its completion by at least two years to 2034.
The agreement, which gives fresh momentum to the Northern Metropolis project, involves HK$39.05 billion in financing from land premiums for the construction of three stations along the Northern Link main line, as well as the design of the spur line, tunnels and other railway facilities. It is the first part of a two-stage deal.
First announced in the 2021 policy address, the Northern Metropolis development aims to transform 30,000 hectares (74,130 acres) in the New Territories into an economic powerhouse featuring a new IT hub and a university town with high-quality living spaces.

data.title: China‚Äôs Baidu merges key app businesses to drive consumer AI push
data.description:
Baidu has merged two of its consumer app businesses into a new artificial intelligence-focused unit, stepping up its push into China‚Äôs fast-evolving and fiercely contested market for consumer AI applications.
The company combined its online document-sharing business Wenku and consumer-oriented cloud storage operation Wangpan to form the Personal Super Intelligence Business Group (PSIG), according to a source familiar with the matter. The move marks one of Baidu‚Äôs largest reorganisations in the past two years.
PSIG would be headed by Wang Ying, a company vice-president who oversaw the two separate businesses prior to the change, the source added. The set-up was first reported by local business and financial outlet Caijing, citing sources who said Wang would report directly to Robin Li Yanhong, company founder and CEO.
Baidu did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The move comes amid intensifying competition in China‚Äôs consumer AI sector, as Big Tech and AI specialist firms race to turn their services and apps into the next ‚Äúsuper app‚Äù.

The restructuring highlighted the new unit‚Äôs strategic role in Baidu‚Äôs AI commercialisation push, as its traditional advertising revenue declined and users increasingly turned to chatbot apps to conduct searches, according to Zhang Yi, founder and chief analyst at consultancy iiMedia.
Zhang said embedding AI more deeply into services such as Wenku and Wangpan ‚Äì both of which charge premium features ‚Äì could lift their paying user base. A larger customer pool would also generate more data to refine Baidu‚Äôs personalised search and content-generation tools.
Wenku and Wangpan rank among Baidu‚Äôs most popular consumer-facing services, with both undergoing AI upgrades in recent years to add capabilities from generating charts and slide decks to drafting reports from simple prompts.
Wenku now hosted more than 1.8 billion documents across a wide range of industries and topics and had 97 million monthly users, while Wangpan had more than 200 million monthly users, including 80 million who used its AI additions, according to data from the company.
Baidu‚Äôs doubling down on consumer AI applications comes as other Big Tech firms shift focus from competing on model performance to embedding AI into their businesses.
Earlier this month, Alibaba Group Holding announced a major update to its flagship Qwen AI app, connecting it to its wider portfolio of services. Alibaba owns the Post.
Companies are also ramping up promotion of their AI apps amid intensifying competition. Tencent said on Sunday it would spend 1 billion yuan ($143 million) on its Lunar New Year campaign, while Baidu had set aside 500 million yuan, as both sought to leverage the holiday to draw in new users to their respective AI chatbot apps, Yuanbao and Wenxin.

data.title: Alibaba‚Äôs Qwen-3 becomes world‚Äôs first AI model to operate in orbit
data.description:
Alibaba Cloud‚Äôs Qwen-3 became the world‚Äôs first general-purpose artificial intelligence model to be uploaded and operated in orbit, marking a milestone in China‚Äôs bid to lead the nascent space-based computing sector.
Chinese aerospace start-up Adaspace Technology successfully deployed Qwen-3 to a space computing centre in orbit where it executed multiple inference tasks in November, according to company executive vice-president Wang Yabo, who spoke at a conference on Monday, as reported by the Star Market Daily.
Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud computing services unit of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the South China Morning Post.
Wang said Qwen-3 was deployed in Adaspace‚Äôs initial space computing centre that formed part of the firm‚Äôs ‚ÄúStar-Compute Project‚Äù, a 2,800-satellite network designed to power physical AI and support AI model training and inference.
The entire Qwen-3 process ‚Äì from uploading queries from the ground to performing on-orbit inference and sending results back to Earth ‚Äì was completed in less than two minutes, according to Wang.
Qwen-3, released in April last year, strengthened the position of Alibaba Cloud‚Äôs Qwen family as the world‚Äôs largest open-source AI ecosystem, surpassing Meta Platforms‚Äô Llama community.
Adaspace‚Äôs initial space computing centre, which was launched into space in May last year, represented the world‚Äôs first AI computing satellite constellation, comprising 12 satellites, according to a Securities Times report.
The company‚Äôs Qwen-3 operation underscored China‚Äôs big head start in orbital data processing, which takes advantage of the unlimited solar energy and lower cooling demands in space, falling launch costs and reduced data transmission costs, while bypassing the physical bottlenecks of ground-based computing.

The second and third space computing centres from Adaspace were currently in production and scheduled for orbital deployment in 2026, according to a report by Jiemian News.
The entire Star-Compute Project will include 2,400 inference satellites and 400 training units deployed in low-earth orbit, with full deployment expected to be completed by 2035.
Founded in 2018, Adaspace had applied for an initial public offering on the Hong Kong stock exchange. Headquartered in Chengdu, the capital of southwestern Sichuan province, the company was valued at over 6.76 billion yuan (US$972 million) after its latest financing round in July last year, according to a Securities Times report.
Adaspace‚Äôs business model covered the entire satellite life cycle, from design, research and development, manufacturing and launch coordination to in-orbit operations and satellite-based solutions.
The company had completed 14 space missions to date, including the development and launch of 33 satellites and payloads, according to Jiemian News.
China has designated aerospace as a strategic priority in its 15th five-year plan through 2030. Fuelled by Beijing‚Äôs policy support, the domestic satellite industry was expected to grow steadily and reach 266.1 billion yuan by 2029 from around 82.7 billion yuan in 2024, according to data from the Satellite Industry Association and Frost & Sullivan.
China and the US currently compete head-to-head in orbital launches. In 2025, the US conducted 180 orbital launches, followed by China‚Äôs 93, according to data from Gunter‚Äôs Space Page.

Chinese aerospace start-up Adaspace Technology successfully deployed Qwen-3 to a space computing centre in orbit where it executed multiple inference tasks in November, according to company executive vice-president Wang Yabo, who spoke at a conference on Monday, as reported by the Star Market Daily.
Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud computing services unit of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the South China Morning Post.
Wang said Qwen-3 was deployed in Adaspace‚Äôs initial space computing centre that formed part of the firm‚Äôs ‚ÄúStar-Compute Project‚Äù, a 2,800-satellite network designed to power physical AI and support AI model training and inference.
The entire Qwen-3 process ‚Äì from uploading queries from the ground to performing on-orbit inference and sending results back to Earth ‚Äì was completed in less than two minutes, according to Wang.
Qwen-3, released in April last year, strengthened the position of Alibaba Cloud‚Äôs Qwen family as the world‚Äôs largest open-source AI ecosystem, surpassing Meta Platforms‚Äô Llama community.
Adaspace‚Äôs initial space computing centre, which was launched into space in May last year, represented the world‚Äôs first AI computing satellite constellation, comprising 12 satellites, according to a Securities Times report.
The company‚Äôs Qwen-3 operation underscored China‚Äôs big head start in orbital data processing, which takes advantage of the unlimited solar energy and lower cooling demands in space, falling launch costs and reduced data transmission costs, while bypassing the physical bottlenecks of ground-based computing.

The second and third space computing centres from Adaspace were currently in production and scheduled for orbital deployment in 2026, according to a report by Jiemian News.
The entire Star-Compute Project will include 2,400 inference satellites and 400 training units deployed in low-earth orbit, with full deployment expected to be completed by 2035.
Founded in 2018, Adaspace had applied for an initial public offering on the Hong Kong stock exchange. Headquartered in Chengdu, the capital of southwestern Sichuan province, the company was valued at over 6.76 billion yuan (US$972 million) after its latest financing round in July last year, according to a Securities Times report.
Adaspace‚Äôs business model covered the entire satellite life cycle, from design, research and development, manufacturing and launch coordination to in-orbit operations and satellite-based solutions.
The company had completed 14 space missions to date, including the development and launch of 33 satellites and payloads, according to Jiemian News.
China has designated aerospace as a strategic priority in its 15th five-year plan through 2030. Fuelled by Beijing‚Äôs policy support, the domestic satellite industry was expected to grow steadily and reach 266.1 billion yuan by 2029 from around 82.7 billion yuan in 2024, according to data from the Satellite Industry Association and Frost & Sullivan.
China and the US currently compete head-to-head in orbital launches. In 2025, the US conducted 180 orbital launches, followed by China‚Äôs 93, according to data from Gunter‚Äôs Space Page.

data.title: On This Day|Hong Kong‚Äôs Cathay Pacific rebrands Dragonair subsidiary in 2016 ‚Äì SCMP archive
data.description:
This article was first published on January 27, 2016
Exit the Dragon¬≠air as Cathay rebrands
by Sijia Jiang
Cathay Pacific group is likely to announce a rebrand¬≠ing of its Dragon¬≠air sub¬≠si¬≠di¬≠ary as ‚ÄúCathay Dragon‚Äù as the com¬≠pany seeks to strengthen its cor¬≠por¬≠ate iden¬≠tity.
The idea to strengthen the sis¬≠ter air¬≠line‚Äôs asso¬≠ci¬≠ation with the premium air¬≠line Cathay Pacific Air¬≠ways has been under dis¬≠cus¬≠sion for a few months, sources famil¬≠iar with the mat¬≠ter told the South China Morn¬≠ing Post.
Cathay‚Äôs man¬≠ager of cor¬≠por¬≠ate com¬≠mu¬≠nic¬≠a¬≠tions, Car¬≠o¬≠lyn Leung, declined to com¬≠ment on whether the com¬≠pany had any rebrand¬≠ing plans for Dragon¬≠air.
‚ÄúFol¬≠low¬≠ing the suc¬≠cess¬≠ful launch of Cathay Pacific‚Äôs new brand ethos and refreshed cor¬≠por¬≠ate iden¬≠tity, we have been review¬≠ing Dragon¬≠air‚Äôs over¬≠all brand pro¬≠pos¬≠i¬≠tion. We will provide more inform¬≠a¬≠tion as appro¬≠pri¬≠ate.‚Äù

The move would put an end to the Dragon¬≠air name which was foun¬≠ded by tex¬≠tile mag¬≠nate Chow Kuang Piu in 1985. The air¬≠line was acquired by Cathay in 2006 fol¬≠low¬≠ing a major share¬≠hold¬≠ing realign¬≠ment involving Cathay‚Äôs par¬≠ent Swire Pacific and the Air China Group. Dragon¬≠air, which has con¬≠tin¬≠ued to oper¬≠ate as a sep¬≠ar¬≠ate air¬≠line within the group with its own visual iden¬≠tity fea¬≠tur¬≠ing a red dragon, mainly flies to main¬≠land China and else¬≠where in Asia. Its fares are cheaper than Cathay. Sixty per cent of its pas¬≠sen¬≠gers hold Hong Kong or main¬≠land Chinese pass¬≠ports accord¬≠ing to the com¬≠pany.
Will Hor¬≠ton, senior ana¬≠lyst with the Centre for Avi¬≠ation, said renam¬≠ing Dragon¬≠air ‚ÄúCathay Dragon‚Äù would be a good move at the right time, as the Dragon¬≠air brand has weak pas¬≠sen¬≠ger recog¬≠ni¬≠tion bey¬≠ond Greater China and lacked brand asso¬≠ci¬≠ation with the lar¬≠ger Cathay group.
‚ÄúWhat is crit¬≠ical is that it is not a full-on mer¬≠ger. Main¬≠tain¬≠ing a sep¬≠ar¬≠ate regional unit will allow the group to lever¬≠age the stronger and global Cathay brand, while at the same time main¬≠tain¬≠ing Dragon¬≠air‚Äôs traffic rights and lower oper¬≠at¬≠ing cost.‚Äù
The rebrand¬≠ing is expec¬≠ted to affect the air¬≠line‚Äôs visual iden¬≠tity across the board ‚Äì most not¬≠ably the air¬≠craft liv¬≠ery. But it is not expec¬≠ted to affect Dragon¬≠air‚Äôs hold¬≠ing of a sep¬≠ar¬≠ate air oper¬≠ator‚Äôs cer¬≠ti¬≠fic¬≠ate, which allows the group to max¬≠im¬≠ise traffic rights usage.
Hor¬≠ton said the move would fol¬≠low a trend among Asian air¬≠line groups with mul¬≠tiple units start¬≠ing to bet¬≠ter lever¬≠age their brands.
‚ÄúThis comes as Asia‚Äôs air¬≠line fam¬≠il¬≠ies start to stitch together their air¬≠lines and become integ¬≠rated groups. Asia‚Äôs ‚Äòair¬≠line‚Äô con¬≠glom¬≠er¬≠ates have had many air¬≠line licences that they haven‚Äôt neces¬≠sar¬≠ily used cohes¬≠ively.‚Äù
Since brand cohe¬≠sion was inev¬≠it¬≠able, it is bet¬≠ter to do it earlier than later when more pas¬≠sen¬≠gers have to be re-edu¬≠cated,‚Äù he said.
Cathay Pacific Group later confirmed the news. This article was first published on January 29, 2016.
Dragon¬≠air found¬≠ing fam¬≠ily ‚Äòsad‚Äô to see brand change
by Sijia Jiang
The end of three dec¬≠ades of the Dragon¬≠air name and the iconic red dragon logo that adorned the planes of Hong Kong‚Äôs first local air¬≠line has stirred fond memor¬≠ies for the daugh¬≠ter of the car¬≠rier‚Äôs founder.
The air¬≠line‚Äôs cur¬≠rent owner, Cathay Pacific Group, announced on January 28, 2016 that Dragon¬≠air will be renamed Cathay Dragon, as repor¬≠ted by the South China Morn¬≠ing Post on January 26, 2016.
‚ÄúThe name change is news to me. However, under¬≠stand¬≠ing how big cor¬≠por¬≠a¬≠tions work, it does not sur¬≠prise me at all,‚Äù Diana Chou, daugh¬≠ter of founder Chao Kuang-piu, told the Post.
‚ÄúIf rebrand¬≠ing can enhance brand equity value, I am sure share¬≠hold¬≠ers will vote for it,‚Äù said Chou, who remains influ¬≠en¬≠tial in Hong Kong avi¬≠ation circles and runs her own private jet busi¬≠ness called L‚Äôvoy¬≠age.
‚ÄúNev¬≠er¬≠the¬≠less, it is sad that such decision has been made as I have very fond memor¬≠ies of how involved my father was in design¬≠ing and pick¬≠ing the name and logo. Our fam¬≠ily exited Dragon¬≠air many years ago. Regard¬≠less of the changes, we will always be remembered as the founders of the com¬≠pany,‚Äù she said.
Dragon¬≠air, which became a wholly-owned unit of Cathay in 2006, has kept its brand iden¬≠tity largely unchanged for nearly 31 years since it was foun¬≠ded by tex¬≠tile mag¬≠nate Chao in May 1985 to chal¬≠lenge the Swire Group air¬≠line.
Its Chinese-themed liv¬≠ery was a stark con¬≠trast to the green of Cathay. The dragon mark will make way for Cathay Pacific‚Äôs brush¬≠wing design, although the red col¬≠our¬≠ing will be retained.
Cathay, together with Citic Pacific and Swire Group, bought 89 per cent of Dragon¬≠air in 1990 before even¬≠tu¬≠ally tak¬≠ing it over for HK$8.22 bil¬≠lion in 2006.
‚ÄúIt is import¬≠ant to note that we are not mer¬≠ging the air¬≠lines. Cathay Pacific and Cathay Dragon will con¬≠tinue to be two sep¬≠ar¬≠ate premium air¬≠lines in the group, both oper¬≠at¬≠ing under their own licences,‚Äù the group‚Äôs chief exec¬≠ut¬≠ive, Ivan Chu, said in unveil¬≠ing the plan. He added that HK$100 mil¬≠lion would be spent to pro¬≠mote the Cathay Dragon brand.
He said the com¬≠pany had weighed the com¬≠mer¬≠cial, legal and reg¬≠u¬≠lat¬≠ory impact of dif¬≠fer¬≠ent options and con¬≠cluded this was the best way to lever¬≠age the strength of both brands in the grow¬≠ing air travel mar¬≠ket in and out of China.
‚ÄúWe believe Dragon¬≠air has strong brand equity in China. Cathay Pacific actu¬≠ally needs to lever¬≠age the Dragon¬≠air brand in China. By 2020, 200 mil¬≠lion Chinese are expec¬≠ted to fly out of China,‚Äù he said.
‚ÄúBut also bear in mind, 130 mil¬≠lion are expec¬≠ted to fly into China from dif¬≠fer¬≠ent parts of the world, so we would like the Cathay brand to lend its strength to Dragon¬≠air,‚Äù he added.
He added that it was ‚Äúout of con¬≠sid¬≠er¬≠a¬≠tion‚Äù to make Cathay Dragon a low-cost sub¬≠si¬≠di¬≠ary.
There will be no changes to the air¬≠line‚Äôs cabin crew uni¬≠forms, and the first seven planes out of its fleet of 41 will be repainted this year, the com¬≠pany said.

data.title: How China‚Äôs AI video generator Kling challenges Google‚Äôs Veo, OpenAI‚Äôs Sora
data.description:
As AI video generation has evolved from a curiosity to a productivity tool embraced by serious creators, the Kling platform developed by China‚Äôs Kuaishou has landed firmly in the top tier alongside Google‚Äôs Veo and OpenAI‚Äôs Sora.
Kling has rapidly grown into a meaningful new business line for the company ‚Äì long a runner-up to TikTok owner ByteDance in China‚Äôs short video arena ‚Äì since its launch in June 2024, reaching about 12 million monthly active users and annual recurring revenue of roughly US$240 million.
The figures are hardly surprising at a time when dancing puppies, alien invasions and digital human live streaming hosts are already everyday scroll-past content, and ‚ÄúAI slop‚Äù has become a common concern. While Kling‚Äôs success is welcome news for Kuaishou, the question is why Kling has become such a hit.
The tool‚Äôs latest metrics, from sources and company announcements, show its momentum. It garnered more than US$20 million in revenue in December alone, bringing it to US$140 million for the year, more than double the US$60 million target Kuaishou set internally at the start of 2025. In January, average daily revenue jumped about 30 per cent from a month earlier.
That traction has lifted Kuaishou‚Äôs long-sluggish Hong Kong shares, which gained 23.3 per cent over the past month to close at HK$78.60 on Monday.
Kuaishou positions Kling as the new poster child for the generative-AI era. The Beijing-based company set up a stand-alone business unit in April 2025 for the tool, and executives have repeatedly pointed to its rising user base and revenue in earnings calls over the past year.
JPMorgan Chase, in a January research note, highlighted Kling‚Äôs monetisation potential, forecasting 1.7 billion yuan (US$244 million) in revenue in 2026 and calling Kuaishou one of the ‚Äúmost undervalued AI stocks globally‚Äù.
Leading AI models, strong interactive design and deep engagement with the creator ecosystem were the three factors underpinning the video generator‚Äôs breakout, according to Zeng Yushen, head of operations for Kling.
‚ÄúA very clear signal last year from the industry was the embrace of AI,‚Äù Zeng said in an interview earlier this month at the Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas. ‚ÄúMeanwhile, real-world use cases have been quickly expanding.‚Äù
Kling rolled out seven upgrades to its foundational models last year. These included Kling O1, which the company touts as the industry‚Äôs first unified multimodal video model, capable of handling images, video, subjects and text within a single prompt, while also allowing on-the-fly editing in post production. Another update, Kling 2.6, significantly improved audio generation.
As rankings across video-generation benchmarks continue to shift, Kling has landed firmly in the top tier alongside Google‚Äôs Veo and OpenAI‚Äôs Sora.

As a tool, Kling leans hard into ease of use and alleviating user pain points. For example, it integrates DeepSeek‚Äôs model to help with prompt writing. A feature called motion control, which lets uploaded subjects mirror the movement in a reference video, fuelled viral trends such as dancing puppies and drew new users to the platform. Another function, dubbed Smart Canvas, combines generation and editing in a single workspace and also enables real-time collaboration.
Kling targets professional creators and enterprise customers, who tend to have a higher willingness to pay than average consumers. These users, through tiered memberships and application programming interface access, account for about 70 per cent of total revenue, the company said. Kuashiao plans to push Kling to the broader consumer market later.
Compared with many leading global models, ‚ÄúKling‚Äôs taste feels closer to what Chinese creators want,‚Äù said Li Brian, a television director at a regional broadcaster who uses AI in tasks from brainstorming to visual-effects production.
Kling‚Äôs Zeng said professional creators shared a common set of needs, including clarity, controllability and consistency, which shaped Kling‚Äôs decision to launch globally from day one. About 70 per cent of its revenue now came from overseas markets, with the US accounting for roughly a third, according to Sensor Tower data.
Operationally and in marketing, however, Kling took a more localised approach, closely tracking regional social-media trends, creator communities and production studios, Zeng said.
Li cautioned that his endorsement of Kling was ‚Äútemporary‚Äù. AI tools were evolving so quickly, he said, that industry insiders were shifting from one platform to another, with little loyalty so far.
Zhong Zhong, a Shanghai-based film producer, said she currently preferred Sora 2, released in September, citing its stronger performance in maintaining character consistency during pre-production tasks such as previsualisation and scene testing.
For now, no single player has established an undisputed lead. Kling‚Äôs Chinese peers, including ByteDance‚Äôs Jimeng, Alibaba‚Äôs Wan and Tencent‚Äôs Hunyuan, as well as start-ups such as Minimax‚Äôs Hailuo, PixVerse and Shengshu‚Äôs Vidu, are all racing to compete.
None of the tools were good enough yet, Zhong said, pointing to persistent weaknesses in shot continuity, the realism of performances and the handling of emotion.
Even so, a consensus is taking hold that AI adoption is inevitable. As AI-generated content floods the market, creators are being pushed to reconsider not just how they will use these tools, but also how to revalue themselves.

data.title: China‚Äôs Anta snags Puma from France‚Äôs Pinault family in US$1.8 billion deal
data.description:
Anta Sports, China‚Äôs largest sportswear firm, is acquiring 29 per cent of German brand Puma from Groupe Artemis of the Pinault family, one of France‚Äôs wealthiest, for ‚Ç¨1.5 billion (US$1.8 billion).
The Fujian-headquartered group said on Tuesday the deal would ‚Äúaccelerate‚Äù its multi-brand globalisation strategy, adding it had no plans to pursue a full takeover or make an offer for Puma Hong Kong. The deal is expected to be completed by the end of the year.
‚ÄúThis acquisition makes Anta Sports the largest shareholder of Puma and marks a major step forward in our ‚Äòsingle-focus, multi-brand, globalisation‚Äô strategy,‚Äù said Ding Shizhong, chairman of the group.
‚ÄúPuma is an iconic global brand with substantial heritage. Working with Puma, we look forward to learning from each other and joining hands to fully unlock the brand‚Äôs full potential.‚Äù
The transaction adds to Anta‚Äôs string of acquisitions since 2018, including the US$6.4 billion purchase of Finland-based Amer Sports ‚Äì which owns Arc‚Äôteryx, Salomon and Wilson ‚Äì Chinese athleisure brand Maia Active, and German outdoor label Jack Wolfskin, acquired for US$250 million.

The Pinault family, which controls global luxury group Kering ‚Äì owner of brands like Bottega Veneta, Gucci and Saint Laurent ‚Äì acquired Puma‚Äôs controlling stake in 2007. Since then it has reduced its stake but remained its single largest shareholder.
Anta‚Äôs acquisition would be financed entirely with internal cash resources, the group said.
Puma is recognised worldwide for its sports and casual footwear, including Puma Clyde, and for collaborations with global celebrities like Blackpink‚Äôs Rose, and pop superstars Rihannna and Dua Lipa, among others. Its key sports markets include Europe, Latin America, Africa and India.
Its product line spans diversified sporting activities such as football, running, training, basketball and motorsport.
‚ÄúAnta has always admired Puma‚Äôs long-term brand value and potential,‚Äù Ding said. ‚ÄúSuch a strong brand DNA and value heritage are rare to come by.‚Äù
Moving forward, the group hoped to ‚Äúbuild strong trust, work together, and leverage our complementary strengths without compromising independence‚Äù, Ding said. ‚ÄúWe look forward to supporting the brand‚Äôs ongoing revival.‚Äù
Anta, founded in 1991, has been expanding operations across key markets, including Southeast Asia, the Middle East, Africa, North America and Europe.

data.title: Geely-linked veteran Yin Qi joins Chinese AI start-up StepFun as chairman
data.description:
Chinese artificial intelligence start-up StepFun has appointed Yin Qi, the co-founder and former CEO of Megvii, as its chairman, as the company wraps up a 5 billion yuan (US$690 million) Series B+ funding round and sharpens its focus on so-called ‚Äúphysical AI‚Äù ‚Äì artificial intelligence designed to power real-world machines such as cars and robots.
Yin, a prominent figure from China‚Äôs first wave of computer-vision start-ups, is also chairman of Qianli Technology, a Geely-backed smart-driving firm.
The appointment reinforced StepFun‚Äôs ‚ÄúAI plus terminal devices‚Äù strategy, the company said, leveraging Yin‚Äôs experience in commercialising AI technologies, particularly in smart cars.
Founded in 2023, Shanghai-based StepFun was set up by Jiang Daxin, former chief scientist at Microsoft Research Asia.
The company said its core management team would consist of Yin as chairman, Jiang as CEO, and Zhu Yibo, a former Microsoft and ByteDance AI veteran, as chief technology officer.
Yi would be responsible for setting the company‚Äôs overall strategic pace and technological direction, the company said in a statement.
‚ÄúQianli is building AI-enabled application scenarios centred on vehicles,‚Äù Yin said in a recent podcast. ‚ÄúStepFun, meanwhile, provides the underlying foundation models ‚Äì the ‚Äòbrain‚Äô and core AI capabilities that support.‚Äù
Qianli‚Äôs Shanghai-listed shares rose about 4.4 per cent on Monday to close at 12 yuan.
Yin, 38, took on the chairmanship of Chongqing Lifan Technology, a local motorcycle and carmaker, in late 2024, following a bankruptcy restructuring that brought backing from Chongqing municipal government and auto group Geely as key shareholders.
As the auto giant Geely sought to strengthen its intelligent-driving capabilities, Yin was brought in as a shareholder and gradually came to manage the company.

Meanwhile, Yin‚Äôs first venture, Megvii, once one of China‚Äôs most high-profile AI stars during the computer-vision boom, shelved its long-running initial public offering plans after a three-year bumpy journey, withdrawing the application in November 2024.
Lifan rebranded as Qianli Technology in February 2025.
Over the past year, Qianli has announced multiple collaborations with Geely to deploy its intelligent driving systems across the carmaker‚Äôs models, striking a ‚Äútechnical partnership‚Äù with StepFun.
The company filed for a Hong Kong listing in October and in December secured Mercedes-Benz‚Äôs China subsidiary as a strategic investor.
Alongside Yin‚Äôs appointment, StepFun, dubbed one of China‚Äôs ‚ÄúAI tigers‚Äù, said it had closed a Series B+ round, following a several-hundred-million-dollar Series B raise at the end of 2024.
The latest financing was backed by state-linked capital including Shanghai State-owned Capital Leading Fund, Pudong Venture Capital and Xuhui Capital, Wuxi‚Äôs Liangxi district fund and state-owned Xiamen ITG.
Other participants included China Life Private Equity Investment, electronics manufacturer Huaqin, and existing investors such as Tencent Holdings, Qiming Venture Partners and 5Y Capital.
Investment in China‚Äôs foundation-model sector remains red hot.
Zhipu AI and MiniMax raised HK$4.3 billion (US$552 million) and HK$4.8 billion, respectively, in Hong Kong listings in January, while Moonshot AI, the maker of chatbot Kimi, raised US$500 million in a recent Series C round, pushing post-valuation to about US$4.3 billion.

data.title: FSDC urges Hong Kong to focus on Africa-Asia financial corridor
data.description:
Hong Kong should focus on connecting its capital markets with African financial centres so that companies and Belt and Road Initiative projects on the continent can raise funds in the city, according to a council that advises the government on development strategy.
In addition to being a funding source for commercial enterprises and infrastructure projects, Hong Kong could be a place for wealthy Africans to set up family offices to manage their wealth, succession and charitable activities, the Financial Services Development Council (FSDC) said in a report on Tuesday. Since 2023, the government has introduced a wide range of measures, including tax incentives and an investment migration scheme, to attract family offices.
‚ÄúFor decades, our city has served as the gateway to China and a critical nexus for Asia‚Äôs economic miracle,‚Äù said King Au, executive director of the FSDC. ‚ÄúToday, we witness the dawn of another transformative epoch: the rapid acceleration of Asia-Africa economic integration. This is not a transient trend, but the foundation of a long-term partnership that will redefine global growth in the 21st century.‚Äù
Four leading African markets ‚Äì Kenya, Morocco, Nigeria and Rwanda ‚Äì could act as entry points for Asian and Hong Kong investors, as they have stable economic growth and sound financial infrastructures, the report said.
China tripled investments under the Belt and Road Initiative in Africa to US$61.2 billion in 2025, fuelled by energy and mining projects as well as a push by manufacturers to take advantage of lower tariff regimes amid the US trade war, according to the Australia-based Griffith Asia Institute.
The initiative, spearheaded by the Chinese government and endorsed by President Xi Jinping in late 2013, aims to improve trade and economic integration via large investments in infrastructure across Asia, Europe and Africa.
Hong Kong-listed Zijin Mining spent US$1 billion to acquire Akyem gold mine, one of the largest in Ghana, in October 2024.
Standard Chartered and other Hong Kong lenders are also active in Africa, providing financing to support expanded trade flows between African and Asian companies.
‚ÄúAs global supply chains recalibrate and new growth poles emerge, the Africa-Asia corridor offers a pathway to diversified, resilient and inclusive economic development for both regions,‚Äù the report said. ‚ÄúHong Kong‚Äôs interest in this dynamic is both strategic and intrinsic.‚Äù
Mainland exports to Africa jumped 25.8 per cent to US$225 billion in 2025, far outpacing the 5.4 per cent growth in African exports to China, which reached US$123 billion, according to data from China‚Äôs General Administration of Customs.
China has been deepening trade with Africa to counter escalating trade tensions with the US following President Donald Trump‚Äôs decision to impose higher tariffs on imports of Chinese goods.

Beijing last year introduced a zero-tariff policy that grants duty-free access to its markets for all products from 53 African countries that have diplomatic ties with China.
Africa also wants to improve trade flows. More than 44 markets joined the African Continental Free Trade Area in 2019, creating a unified trading bloc with 1.2 billion consumers. The agreement is part of Agenda 2063, the African Union‚Äôs blueprint for transforming the continent into a global powerhouse.
‚ÄúBy aligning standards and facilitating seamless capital flows between African markets and strategic Asian hubs such as Hong Kong, we are turning Africa‚Äôs immense potential into tangible, global impact for generations to come,‚Äù said Lamia Merzouki, chair of the World Alliance of International Financial Centres, a Brussels-based non-profit association that promotes cooperation among financial centres.

data.title: Tencent‚Äôs Pony Ma teases new AI social feature for Yuanbao app
data.description:
Pony Ma Huateng, founder and CEO of Chinese tech giant Tencent Holdings, has teased a new artificial intelligence-powered social feature in the company‚Äôs latest push to keep pace in China‚Äôs intensifying AI race.
In an internal address to staff, Ma said Tencent was beta testing a new social tool inside its AI assistant app Yuanbao, and encouraged employees to try it out and help debug the feature, according to Tencent staff who attended the meeting.
The feature, known in Chinese as Yuanbao Pai, integrates AI with online social groups, allowing users to interact and collaborate with friends ‚Äì Tencent‚Äôs latest experiment as it expands the capabilities of its consumer-facing AI app.
Tencent did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Tencent‚Äôs move comes as competition among China‚Äôs Big Tech groups and AI start-ups continues to heat up, with the Shenzhen-based firm still playing catch-up to front runners such as ByteDance and DeepSeek.
ByteDance-owned Doubao remained the mainland‚Äôs top consumer AI app in December, with 155 million weekly active users in the second week last month, according to data from QuestMobile.
That figure was nearly double the 81.56 million recorded by DeepSeek‚Äôs eponymous chatbot, which ranked as the market‚Äôs No 2 player over the same period.
Tencent‚Äôs Yuanbao, Ant Group‚Äôs Ant A-Fu and Alibaba Cloud‚Äôs Qwen app were ranked No 3, 4 and 5, respectively, according to QuestMobile. Ant Group is the fintech affiliate of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the Post.
Ma also defended Tencent‚Äôs measured approach to AI, even as he acknowledged that the emergence of DeepSeek and OpenAI‚Äôs ChatGPT had reshaped the trajectory of AI development.
‚ÄúIt‚Äôs Tencent‚Äôs style to advance steadily and surely,‚Äù Ma told staff at the annual gathering.
He pledged to step up the company‚Äôs long-term investment, saying Tencent had already made sweeping upgrades to its infrastructure and talent pool in the field.
Tencent‚Äôs renewed push has been underscored by its recent recruitment of former OpenAI researcher Vinces Yao Shunyu, who was appointed to lead the company‚Äôs AI infrastructure efforts, including large language model development.
Yao was named chief AI scientist under the CEO‚Äôs office, reporting directly to Tencent president Martin Lau Chi-ping.
With Yao‚Äôs appointment, Ma said Tencent had shifted its focus towards hiring young, ‚ÄúAI-native‚Äù talent, with the goal of ‚Äúrebuilding Tencent‚Äôs AI research and development from the ground up‚Äù, according to attendees.
On Sunday, Tencent also escalated its consumer AI push by launching a 1 billion yuan (US$137 million) hong bao giveaway campaign through Yuanbao.
Ma said he hoped the initiative could replicate the success of WeChat‚Äôs red packet feature, which became an overnight sensation during the Spring Festival in 2014.

data.title: Hong Kong model Abby Choi‚Äôs murder trial set for September
data.description:
Abby Choi Tin-fung‚Äôs ex-husband, his brother and father will stand trial for her alleged killing in September, three years after the death of the Hong Kong model.
In a closed-door hearing on Tuesday, Alex Kwong Kong-chi, his elder brother Anthony Kwong Kong-kit and father Kwong Kau appeared before Deputy High Court Judge Brian Keith for the first time.
The pre-trial hearing was held for the judge to give directions to the parties regarding the trial.
According to the judiciary‚Äôs website, the trio did not have legal representation at Tuesday‚Äôs hearing. They will return to court for a second pre-trial hearing on February 27.
The trial is set to begin on September 21 and is expected to last 45 days at the High Court.
Choi‚Äôs death shocked the city in February 2023, when her body parts were found in a flat inside a three-storey house in Lung Mei Tsuen, Tai Po.
The defendants have been remanded in custody since their arrests that same month.
The high-profile murder case then entered a lengthy legal process, with multiple adjournments over the years before being transferred to the High Court for trial.
The defendants were previously represented by lawyer Eric So Chi-kit during earlier hearings.
In a separate case in June 2024, Alex Kwong was sentenced to 3¬Ω years in jail for stealing HK$6.3 million (US$806,860) in gold and jewellery and fleeing before his theft trial began in 2015.
His mother, Jenny Li Sui-heung, was jailed in October 2025 for 1¬Ω years for obstructing a police investigation into Alex Kwong after the model‚Äôs murder. She was found to have prevented police from locating her son and persuaded Choi‚Äôs mother to stay silent about his whereabouts.

data.title: ‚ÄòAll options‚Äô considered amid feedback from flat owners hit by Tai Po fire: Lee
data.description:
The Hong Kong government is analysing feedback from 90 per cent of property owners affected by the devastating Tai Po fire, with the city leader stressing that ‚Äúevery option‚Äù is being considered amid some residents‚Äô demands for in situ redevelopment.
Two months after the fire that claimed 167 lives, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu on Tuesday underlined the importance of announcing a long-term resettlement plan for thousands of displaced residents, but stopped short of providing a timeline.
He spoke to the press shortly after Erick Tsang Kwok-wai stepped down as secretary for constitutional and mainland affairs, citing health issues due to prostate cancer.
Lee reassured the public that investigations by law enforcement agencies and the independent committee looking into the fire were under way at ‚Äúfull speed‚Äù, reiterating the government‚Äôs commitment to uncovering the truth and identifying responsible parties as soon as possible.
The fire on November 26 engulfed seven of the eight high-rise residential buildings in Wang Fuk Court, displacing nearly 5,000 people, many of whom are currently staying in temporary accommodation arranged by the government.
To gauge residents‚Äô preferences for long-term rehousing solutions, the government previously distributed questionnaires to flat owners. Options included a buyout plan, a flat-for-flat arrangement, and an in situ development plan, which could take up to 10 years to complete.
Lee said ahead of his weekly meeting with top advisers on Tuesday that the government was analysing feedback from 90 per cent of the flat owners, while housing authorities were reviewing existing projects to provide more options.
‚ÄúWe are expediting all work while taking compassion, reason and law into account,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúWe have to make the decision as soon as possible. The sooner the affected families can be rehoused, the better.‚Äù
Lee described the long-term rehousing situation as ‚Äúcomplicated‚Äù, given the diverse circumstances and needs of displaced residents.

Deputy Financial Secretary Michael Wong Wai-lun, who leads a government task force on emergency resettlement for the residents, earlier shifted his stance on in situ redevelopment. The option gained support among some residents, who voiced their preference in public petitions.
Wong initially described in situ redevelopment as ‚Äúnot practical‚Äù. He later acknowledged residents‚Äô wishes but also stressed the significant challenges involved.
While Lee did not provide a specific timeline for the announcement of the resettlement plan, he stressed that ‚Äúevery option‚Äù was being weighed. He said the government must also carefully consider the use of public resources, legal issues and property rights.
He added that authorities would ‚Äúpursue accountability to the very end‚Äù, ensuring that all responsible parties, whether they were government or private entities, would be held accountable.
